coredata.dc:title: Determination of multiple targets by using dual-fluorescence emissive carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.
coredata.dc:creator: MAO, Quan-Xing
YANG, Jun-Na
WU, Xi
FU, Yang
SONG, Xi-Ming
MA, Tian-Yi
coredata.dc:description: There are various kinds of pollutants in our environment from the discharged sewage that damage human’s health. Determination of multiple pollutants is important for safety and the detection methods are always needed. This study reports a determination method for p-nitrophenol (p-np) and copper ions by using the dual fluorescence centers of N,S co-doped CDs (N,S-CDs). N,S-CDs was prepared by hydrothermal treatment of thiourea and citric acid in NN dimethylformamide. The as-prepared N,S-CDs are spherical particles with size distribution of 1.20±0.42 nm, as well as functional groups such as thiol and carboxyl. The rich surface species endow the CDs dual fluorescence centers at 380 nm/460 nm and 550 nm/625 nm. Interestingly, the fluorescence at 380 nm/460 nm can be quenched by p-np, while the fluorescence at 550/625 nm can be quenched by copper ions. Mechanism investigations revealed that inner filter effect of p-np and combination with copper ions are responsible for the fluorescence quenching. Determination methods are thus found with detection limits of 6.47×10−6 mol L− 1 for p-np and 6.0×10−7 mol L− 1 for Cu2+. Piped water are tested and the results are satisfactory with recoveries in the range of 94.33%—100.67% for p-np and 98.67%—106.33% for Cu2+. In conclusion, this work provides a new approach for determination of multiple targets by using the dual fluorescence of N,S-CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,S co-doped carbon dots
Dual fluorescence
p-nitrophenol
Copper ions
Detection of multiple targets

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of the N,S-CDs
The optical properties of the N,S-CDs
Possible mechanism for sensing Cu2+ and p-np

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21804062 and 52071171 ), Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program - Pan Deng Scholars (XLYC1802005), Liaoning BaiQianWan Talents Program (LNBQW2018B0048), Natural Science Fund of Liaoning Province for Excellent Young Scholars (2019-YQ-04), Key Project of Scientific Research of the Education Department of Liaoning Province (LZD201902), Shenyang Science and Technology Project ( 21–108–9–04 ), Australian Research Council (ARC) through Future Fellowship (FT210100298, FT210100806), Discovery Project (DP220100603), Linkage Project (LP210100467, LP210200504, LP210200345), and Industrial Transformation Training center (IC180100005) schemes, CSIRO Energy center and Kick-Start Project. The Study Melbourne Research Partnerships program has been made possible by funding from the Victorian Government through Study Melbourne.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The illustration for the preparation of N,S-CQDs; (b) TEM images of the N,S-CDs; (c) The corresponding particles size distribution.
(a) Raw XPS spectrum of the N,S-CDs; High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (b), N 1s (c), O 1s (d) and S 2p (e); (f) FT-IR spectrum of N,S-CDs.
(a) UV–Vis absorption of N,S-CDs; (b) Excitation-dependent fluorescence feature of N,S-CDs; FL spectra of the N,S-CDs at different pH values excited at 380 nm (c) and 550 nm (d).

The response to Cu2+ of the fluorescence with excitation/emission wavelengths at 550 nm/625 nm (a) and 380 nm/460 nm (c); The liner calibration curve for Cu2+ at 550 nm/625 nm (b); The fluorescence intensity variation at 380 nm/460 nm in the presence of Cu2+ (d); The fluorescence response of the N,S-CDs (550 nm/625 nm) in the presence of different interferences (500 μM) (e).

The fluorescence response of N,S-CDs to p-np with excitation/emission wavelengths at 380 nm/460 nm (a) and 550 nm/625 nm (c); The liner calibration curve for p-np at 380/460 nm (b); The fluorescence intensity variation at 550 nm/625 nm in the presence of p-np (d); The fluorescence response of the N,S-CDs (380 nm/460 nm) in the presence of different interferences (e).
Schematic illustration of the sensing mechanism for Cu2+ and p-np.
Detection of Cu2+ in running water samples. (n = 3, at 95% confidence level).
Detection of p-np in piped samples (n = 3, at 95% confidence level).
Comparison of the LOD of this study and previous reported methods.

row.entry: polydopamine (PDA) film doped with gold nanoparticles

head.ce:title: Determination of multiple targets by using dual-fluorescence emissive carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Clean Energy Chemistry, Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials of Liaoning Province, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Clean Energy Chemistry, Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials of Liaoning Province, College of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aInstitute of Clean Energy Chemistry, Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials of Liaoning Province, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: RMIT University, School of Science, Melbourne, VIC 3000, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bRMIT University, School of Science, Melbourne, VIC 3000, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There are various kinds of pollutants in our environment from the discharged sewage that damage human’s health. Determination of multiple pollutants is important for safety and the detection methods are always needed. This study reports a determination method for p-nitrophenol (p-np) and copper ions by using the dual fluorescence centers of N,S co-doped CDs (N,S-CDs). N,S-CDs was prepared by hydrothermal treatment of thiourea and citric acid in NN dimethylformamide. The as-prepared N,S-CDs are spherical particles with size distribution of 1.20±0.42 nm, as well as functional groups such as thiol and carboxyl. The rich surface species endow the CDs dual fluorescence centers at 380 nm/460 nm and 550 nm/625 nm. Interestingly, the fluorescence at 380 nm/460 nm can be quenched by p-np, while the fluorescence at 550/625 nm can be quenched by copper ions. Mechanism investigations revealed that inner filter effect of p-np and combination with copper ions are responsible for the fluorescence quenching. Determination methods are thus found with detection limits of 6.47×10−6 mol L− 1 for p-np and 6.0×10−7 mol L− 1 for Cu2+. Piped water are tested and the results are satisfactory with recoveries in the range of 94.33%—100.67% for p-np and 98.67%—106.33% for Cu2+. In conclusion, this work provides a new approach for determination of multiple targets by using the dual fluorescence of N,S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Modern industry facilitates high-quality life of people, but also caused environmental pollutions by discharging hazards waste. Although the waste discharge must meet certain standards, trace hazards can be enriched through food chain and cause diseases. For example, if waste copper discharged from melting industry are enriched in human body, Alzheimer, Parkinson diseases and renal failure may occur [1–3]. For another example, p-nitrophenol (p-np) is a widely used chemical reagent to produce dyes, insecticides, and pharmaceuticals [4]. But the discharged p-np into environment can harm human’s central nervous system and vagal nerve endings [5,6]. In reality, most of discharged sewage contains multiple pollutants. Therefore, it is highly desirable to monitor the multiple pollutants, especially for copper ions and p-np due to their high toxicity.

So far, various methods have been developed to determine Cu2+ and p-np. Atomic spectrometry is very useful to determine Cu2+ due to the high accuracy and sensitivity [7]. Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) spectroscopy is recently developed for the determination of trace copper ions on account of its special fingerprint identification capability [8]. Electrochemical method based on polyurethane-doped platinum nanoparticles-coated electrode facilitated sensitive and selective detection of trace amounts of Cu2+ ions in biological and environmental samples [9]. Similarly, voltammetric analysis was effectively implemented to p-nitrophenol detection by using α-Fe2O3@MgAl-CO3-LDH coated electrode [10]. Besides, the phosphorescence of mangnanese-doped ZnS quantum dots have been applied to determine p-np [11]. In addition, a colorimetric quantitative method for p-np have been found based on polydopamine-film-doped gold nanoparticles [12]. Although these methods have made great progress in quantification of Cu2+ and p-np, there are several drawbacks such as the dependent on expensive equipment, convoluted pretreatment, sophisticated preparation process, and single response to targets. New methods with easy preparation procedure, quick and multiple response and good reproducibility are always needed.

Fluorescence spectroscopy is easy and rapid method with high sensitivity. Different fluorescent materials have been developed for analysis of copper and p-np. Molecules, such as commercial reagent Amplex® UltraRed reacted with Cu2+ and produced another fluorescence product. The obtained fluorescence signal was linear with respect to the Cu2+ concentration and the copper ions were thus determined [13]. Besides, DNAzyme self-assembled gold nanoparticles [14], tannic acid-capped copper nanoclusters [15], SiO2-anchored CdS nanocrystals [16] also exhibited different fluorescence responses to copper ions and the concentration of Cu2+ can be analyzed. For determination of p-np, fluorescent materials such as β-cyclodextrin-capped ZnO quantum dots [17], bovine serum albumin capped Ag nanoclusters [18], polydopamine [19], and glutathione-stabilized copper nanoclusters [20] have been developed and used as probes. These fluorescent materials have provided efficient, sensitive, fast and satisfactory analysis for Cu2+ and p-np. However, these probes usually determine copper or p-np singly. A versatile fluorescent probe for determining multiple targets is desirable.

Carbon dots (CDs) are excellent fluorescent probes due to the stable and tunable fluorescence, easy and cheap preparation, which has been used to sense metal ions, pesticide or biomolecules [21–30]. Usually, photo-induced electron/charge transfer, resonance energy transfer, or inner filter effect occur when the targets interact with CDs, and the quenched fluorescence is proportional to the concentration of targets [23]. However, most of the CDs exhibit single fluorescence emission, which allows determination of single target [21,22,24,25]. Although some previously prepared CDs exhibited dual fluorescence emission, the CDs are usually employed as ratiometric probe to determine a single target [29,30]. Previous investigations have proved that the surface states such as groups or element doping regulate the fluorescence emission and the affinity to different targets [33,34]. Therefore, preparing carbon dots with various kinds of surface states and multiple emissions might provide new approaches for determination of multiple targets.

In this study, we prepared nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) with dual fluorescence emission at 380 nm/460 nm and 550 nm/625 nm, which are employed to determine p-np and copper ions, respectively. Notably, the developed method was successfully used for p-np and copper assay in piped water samples, demonstrating the potential of the N,S-CDs for multiple targets determination in environmental monitor.

Thiourea, citric acid, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), p-np, lysine (Lys), glutamic acid (Glc), p-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine, ortho-aminophenol, ortho-nitroaniline, o-nitrophenol, para-hydroxyaniline, 2,2-bis(4-hydroxyphenyl)propane were purchased from Sinopharm. NaCl, KBr, NaCl, PbSO4, AlCl3, CdCl2, FeCl2, Na3PO4, MnSO4, FeCl3, CuCl2, ZnCl2, K2Cr2O7, MgCl2, SnCl2, Na2SO4 and CaCl2 were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained from a JEM-2100 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded on a Spectrum 100 spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Inc, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on an ESCALAB 250Xi analysis system (Thermo Instruments Inc., USA). Zeta potential measurement was conducted by using a nano ZS-90 particle size analyzer (Malvern Instruments, United Kingdom). Fluorescence spectra and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on F-200 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and U-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan).

The N,S-CDs were prepared according to a modified method [26]. Typically, 4.0 g of thiourea and 2.0 g of citric acid were first mixed and dissolved in DMF (20 mL). Then the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, followed by heating at 160 °C for 6 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the raw product was treated by using 40 mL of NaOH solution (50 mg mL−1) while stirring for 10.0 min. Subsequently, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 r min−1 for 30 min. Then the supernatant was collected and dialyzed in deionized water (M w cutoff: 3.5 kDa) for 24 h. After freeze-drying, N,S-CDs powder was obtained and stock solution (1 mg mL−1) was prepared by dissolving the N,S-CDs powder in phosphate buffer solution (0.2 mol L− 1, pH 6.5).

A series of Cu2+ standard solutions with different concentrations were prepared by dissolving CuCl2 in phosphate buffer solution (0.2 mol L− 1, pH 6.5). In a typical measurement process, 500 μL of Cu2+ solution was mixed with 50 μL of N,S-CDs stock solution, and then fluorescence spectra were recorded at excitation/emission wavelength of 550 nm/625 nm.

To test the selectivity of the fluorescence response at 550 nm/625 nm to Cu2+, different metal cations and anions (Pb2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, PO4 3−, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Na+, K +, Ca2+, Cr2O7 2−, Sn2+, Mg2+, SO4 2−, 1000 μmol L− 1) were mixed with N,S-CDs according to the above description and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at the identical conditions.

The possibility of practical application of the N,S-CDs was demonstrated by analysis of Cu2+ in piped water. First, the piped water was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove insoluble matters. Then, the piped water was spiked with different concentrations (1.0, 5.0 and 10.0 μmol L− 1) of standard Cu2+ stock solution. After that, 500 μL of the spiked piped water was mixed with 50 μL of N,S-CDs stock solution, and the fluorescence were recorded at excitation/emission wavelength of 550 nm/625 nm.

A series of p-np standard solutions were prepared by dissolving p-np in phosphate buffer solution (0.2 mol L−1, pH 6.5). Then 200 μL of the p-np standard solution was mixed with 50 μL of N,S-CDs stock solution. Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at excitation/emission wavelength of 380 nm/460 nm.

To evaluate the selectivity of the fluorescence at 380 nm/460 nm of N,S-CDs for p-np, p-n was replaced by several common coexisting molecules or isomers, such as lysine (Lys), glutamic acid (Glc), p-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine, ortho-aminophenol, ortho-nitroaniline, o-nitrophenol, para-hydroxyaniline and 2,2-bis(4-hydroxyphenyl). The concentration of the interferences are 200 μmol L−1. After mixed these interferences with N,S-CDs, the solution was measured at the identical conditions.

To assess the practicability of the proposed method, the contents of p-np in piped water was determined by using N,S-CDs as fluorescent probe. The piped water was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, followed by the addition of different amounts of p-np standard solution (10.0, 50.0 and 150.0 μmol L−1). The spiked samples were measured as described in above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N,S-CDs was prepared by hydrothermal treatment of thiourea and citric acid in NN dimethylformamide at 160 ℃ for 6 h, followed by treatment with NaOH and dialysis. This process was artistically illustrated in Fig. 1a . The morphology of the as-prepared N,S-CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM). As illustrated in Fig. 1b, the N,S-CDs demonstrates monodisperse spherical morphology with lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, which is typical structure of CDs. After counting 100 particles, the N,S-CDs was demonstrated to exhibit narrow size distribution with an average diameter of 1.20 ± 0.42 nm, which is illustrated in Fig. 1c.

The chemical and surface structures of the N,S-CDs were characterized by XPS and FT-IR. As illustrated in Fig. 2a , the raw XPS reveals that N,S-CDs mainly consist of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and sulfur. The high resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2b) reveals peaks at 284.4 eV, 285.0 eV and 287.4 eV, which are corresponded to the C = C/CC, C = N and C = O bonds [35]. In Fig. 2c, the fine spectrum of N demonstrates the presence of pyridinic N at 399.5 eV, pyrollic N at 400.5 eV, and graphitic N at 401.5 eV [36,37]. The O 1s XPS spectrum in Fig. 2d shows two peaks at 531.1 eV and 532.6 eV, which are attributed to COH and C = O. The fine spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 2e) displays peaks at 163.2 eV and 168.1 eV, which are attributed to C-S and NC–S [38,39]. In Fig. 2f, FT-IR spectra of N,S-CDs proves the stretching vibration of N–H and O–H at 3390 cm−1 and 3180 cm−1, as well as the stretching vibrations of the C = O (1620 cm−1), CN (1190 cm−1), CH (773 cm−1) and C–S (646 cm−1) bonds. The structure characterizations prove that the CDs are doped with nitrogen and sulfur, and the surface of CDs are functionalized with abundant groups such as –OH, –HS, –COOH, as well as various chemical bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties of the N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3a , the UV–vis absorption spectra of the N,S-CDs is featured with a narrow peak at 335 nm and a broad peak centered at 537 nm. The absorption at 335 nm is assigned to n-π* transition of C = O/CO, and the absorption at around 537 nm is attributed to the contribution of CN/C = N structures [35,40]. The absorption band at around 537 nm also implies the existence of large sp2 domain or π system [41,42]. Fig. 3b indicates that the N,S-CDs exhibit excitation dependent fluorescence feature in the range of 380–600 nm, and there are two fluorescence centers with maximum excitation/emission wavelengths at 380 nm/460 nm and 550 nm/625 nm.

As far as we know, CDs with intrinsic dual fluorescence emission centering in blue and red light regions are rarely reported [31,32,43-45]. It has been proved that the fluorescence of CDs is controlled by the surface states and graphitic nitrogen triggers red fluorescence emission in carbon dots [33,41]. Herein, the surface states of N,S-CDs are mainly regulated by two kinds of chemical bonds consisting of carbon-oxygen and carbon-nitrogen, i.e., C = O/CO, and C = N/CN, which dominate the blue/green fluorescence center at 380 nm/460 nm and red fluorescence center at 550 nm/625 nm, respectively [46,47]. In addition, the doped sulfur bonds with carbon (C–S, NC–S bonds) and hydrogen (H–S bond) are also considered as contributor for the longer wavelength fluorescence center [38]. The intrinsic dual fluorescence emission provide the N,S-CDs potential in determining multiple targets.

The two fluorescence centers of N,S-CDs response differently to the variation of pH value at the identical measurement conditions. As shown in Fig. 3c and Fig.3d, the fluorescence of at 380 nm/460 nm changed slightly when pH value increased from 3 to 11, while the fluorescence at 550 nm/625 nm increased about 13 folds. As illustrated in above, the fluorescence center at 550 nm/625 nm is regulated by C = N/CN and C-S/NC-S structures, while the fluorescence center at 380 nm/460 nm is controlled by C = O/CO species. According to a recent investigation, carbon-nitrogen/surlfur bonds and related groups are more sensitive to the variation of proton concentration than carbon-oxygen bonds and related groups [35]. This might be the reason for the difference in pH response of the two fluorescnece centers.

In addition, the N,S-CDs exhibit stable fluorescence under continuous irradiation and high ionic strength. As shown in Fig. S1a, b, both of the fluorescence of the two excitation/emission centers are almost unchanged even the irradiation time last for 10000 s. Furthermore, high concentration of salt (up to 0.1 mol L− 1) hardly enhance or quench the fluorescence of the N,S-CDs (Fig. S1c, d). Those results confirmed the eminent photostability of the N,S-CDs, which endows them potential applications in complex samples.

As elucidated above, N,S-CDs exhibites two intrinsic fluorescence centers with maximun excitation/emission wavelengths at 380 nm/460 nm and 550 nm/625 nm. We investigated the fluorescence responses of the two centers upon addition of various concetrations of Cu2+.

As illustrated in Fig. 4a , the fluorescence intensity at 625 nm (excitation wavelength: 550 nm) of N,S-CDs decreases gradually with the increse of Cu2+. Fig. 4b indicates the linear relationship (R 2=0.997) between I/I 0 and the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 0–10 μM, where I and I 0 represent the fluorescence intensities of N,S-CDs in the prescence and absence of Cu2+. The limit of detection (3σ) was calculated to be 6.0 × 10 − 7 mol L− 1. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity at 460 nm (excitation wavelength: 380 nm) changes slightly upon the presence of Cu2+ (Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d), indicating that the fluorescence at shorter wavelength is insensitive to copper ions.

According to the fluorescence responses towards Cu2+, an analytical method can be found based on the fluorescence intensity changes at 550 nm/625 nm. However, an ideal analytical methods for metal ions detection desires not only sensitivity, but also selectivity, especially in complex environmental sample. Thus, selectivity test was carried out before practical application. Several representative metal ions and anions (Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, K +, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Zn2+, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, and p-np) are mixed with N,S-CDs and the fluorescence at 550/625 nm were recorded. As shown in Fig. 4e, these cations, ions and p-np pose little interferences on the determincation of Cu2+, indicating the excellent selectivity of the fluorescence method for determination of Cu2+ at 550 nm/625 nm.

To evaluate the practibility of the N,S-CDs, the content of Cu2+ in real sample, i.e., piped water, was determined. As displayed in Table 1 , the reasults indicate that no Cu2+ exist in the tested samples. Therefore, the standard addition experiments were conducted by spiked with different concentrations of standard Cu2+ solutions (1, 5, 10 μM) into the piped water sample. The measured values are consistent with the spiked values. The recoveries of Cu2+ in real sample ranges from 98.67% to 106.33%. These results show good accuracy and precision of the determination method for Cu2+ in real sample, which demonstrates the prospect of the as-prepared N,S-CDs in the field of environmental analysis.

As illustrated above, there are two fluorescence centers with maximum exitation/emission wavelength at 380 nm/460 nm and 550 nm/625 nm, which response differently towards Cu2+. Actually, the two fluorescence centers response differently to p-np, too. As displayed in Fig. 5a , the fluorescenc intensity at 380 nm/460 nm gradually derceases along with the increasing concentration of p-np. A linear relationship between I/I 0 and the concentration of p-np can be found in the range of 0–150 μM with R 2=0.998 (Fig. 5b), where I 0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs before and after the addition of p-np. The detection limit (3σ) is derived to be 6.47×10−6 mol L− 1. A detection method for p-np can be found according to the linear equation. At the same time, however, there are not obvious fluctuations in fluorescence intensity at 550 nm/625 nm when p-np were added in the solution of N,S-CDs, as shown in Fig. 5c and Fig. 5d. This indicates that the longer wavelength fluorescence center of N,S-CDs are inert about p-np.

The selectivity of the fluorescence at 380 nm/460 nm towards p-np was tested with substances that are common coexisting in real samples or that share similar structure to p-np, such as lysine (Lys), glutamic acid (Glu), p-phenylenediamine (PPD), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), o-aminophenol (OAP), o-nitroaniline, o-nitrophenol (o-np), para-hydroxyaniline (PAP), 2,2-bis(4-hydroxyphenyl)propane (BPA), Na3PO4 and KNO3. As shown in Fig. 5e, no obvious disturbance to the detection of p-np was observed in the presence of these interferences (200 μM). Although o-phenylenediamine and o-nitroaniline quench the fluorescence of N,S-CQDs slightly, they might not interfere the detection of p-np because the concentration of the two substances in real samples are usually low. These result demonstrate the selectivity of N,S-CQDs for p-np.

To evaluate the pracitibility of the N,S-CDs, the concentration of p-np in piped water was tested via the above fluorescence quenching method. No p-np was found in piped water and then spike experiment were performed. As displayed in Table 2
, after spiked with 10 μM, 50 μM, 150 μM of p-np, 9.43 ± 0.99, 49.85 ± 1.22, and 151.00 ± 2.05 μM of p-np were found in piped water. The recoveries range between 94.33% and 100.67%. These results show that the method are accurate with high presision, which is suitable for analysis of p-np in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for sensing Cu2+ and p-np
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the fluorescence of CDs can be quenched by metal ions through various mechanisms, such as Förster resonance energy transfer, photo-induced electron transfer, surface energy transfer, and inner filter effect [48]. However, previous CDs usually equipped with single fluorescence center. In this study, there are two fluorescence centers in the N,S-CDs, which response differently towards Cu2+.

In this sduty, the fluorescence center of 380 nm/460 nm is attributed to the CO/C = O strucures, or carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. The insenstive response of this center towards Cu2+ is similar to a previous report, and this might be due to the weak interation between carbonxyl/hydroxyl groups and Cu2+ [49]. As elucitated above, the fluorescence center of 550 nm/625 nm is origined from the contribution of CN/C = N and C-S structures. Generally, the fluorescence quenching of CDs induced by Cu2+ is attributed to the charge/electron transfer between amino groups and Cu2+ [19,50]. However, the negative surface charge of N,S-CDs in a wide pH value range (Fig. S2) suggests that there are seldome amino groups on the surface of N,S-CDs, which is in accordance with the XPS results that nitrogen mainly form double or triple bonds (Fig. 2c). Therefore, the charge/electron transfer between amino groups and Cu2+ cannot explain the fluorescence quenching, another unusual interactions should be responsible for the fluorescence quenching at 550 nm/625 nm. Considering that –HS groups has been demonstrated on the surface of N,S-CDs, the coordinatioin between thiol groups and Cu2+ is attributed to be the origin of the fluorescence quenching [38]. This can be further proved by the UV–vis absorption spectra of N,S-CDs (Fig. S3a) upon addition of Cu2+, which shows the increase of absorbance with increasing concentration of Cu2+. Moreover, Fig. S3b demonstrates that the Zeta potential of N,S-CDs turns positive when Cu2+ is added, which might be another evidence for the coordinatioin of N,S-CDs and Cu2+.

For the sensing mechanism of p-np, we investigate the UV–vis spectra of p-np and N,S-CDs, as well as the fluorescence excitation spectra of CDs. As shown in Figure S4a, there is a wide overlap between the absorption spectrum of p-np and the excitation spectrum of the N,S-CDs (excitation/emission at 380 nm/460 nm). The result indicates that inner filter effect (IFE) is responsible for the quenching of fluorescence. This can be furthur proved by the UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. S4b), which suggest that the mixture of N,S-CDs and p-np showns almost the identical spectrum to that of p-np. Thus, it can be concluded that the selective detection of p-np is based on fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs induced by IFE.
The different sensing mechanism can be artistically illustrated in Scheme 1 .

Herein, by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and thiourea, we prepared nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) with dual fluorescence emission at 380 nm/460 nm and 550 nm/625 nm. The two fluorescence centers of N,S-CDs are dominated by surface structures, i.e., C = O/CO and C = N/CN, respectively. The fluorescence center at 550 nm/625 nm can be quenched by copper ions due to the combination between thoil groups and copper ions, but the fluorescence is inert about p-nitrophenol. Meanwhile, the fluorescence center of 380 nm/460 nm is quenched by p-np via inner filter effect but can not be quenched by copper ions. This fluorescence feature was employed to determine copper ions and p-np in piped waters and good recoveries can be obtained. This work provides an new sight on the determination of multi targets by using the dual fluorescence feature of N,S-CDs.

Quanxing Mao: conceptualization, writing and revising manuscript, resources; Junna Yang: methodology, formal analysis, investigation, visualization, writing the original manuscript; Xi Wu: validation, formal analysis; Yang Fu: formal analysis, investigation; Ximing Song: conceptualization; Tianyi Ma: resources, writing – review & editing, supervision, project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21804062 and 52071171), Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program - Pan Deng Scholars (XLYC1802005), Liaoning BaiQianWan Talents Program (LNBQW2018B0048), Natural Science Fund of Liaoning Province for Excellent Young Scholars (2019-YQ-04), Key Project of Scientific Research of the Education Department of Liaoning Province (LZD201902), Shenyang Science and Technology Project (21–108–9–04), Australian Research Council (ARC) through Future Fellowship (FT210100298, FT210100806), Discovery Project (DP220100603), Linkage Project (LP210100467, LP210200504, LP210200345), and Industrial Transformation Training center (IC180100005) schemes, CSIRO Energy center and Kick-Start Project. The Study Melbourne Research Partnerships program has been made possible by funding from the Victorian Government through Study Melbourne.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cjac.2023.100227.
